attachments.title: Three Things I Wish I Knew When I Started Designing Languages
attachments.text: Peter Alvaro talks about the reasons one should engage in language design and why many of us would (or should) do something so perverse as to design a language that no one will ever use. He shares some of the extreme and sometimes obnoxious opinions that guided his design process.

I don't agree with Prof. Alvaro that its okay to have only one user for your language. If it is useful, it should be widely used and not an obscurity.  I am not a fan of Datalog and its derivatives. I am solidly in the Prof. Wirth camp, which is that Algorithms + data structures = Programs, and i believe the weak spot in computer science is data structures, as the fundamental algorithms are now fairly well known.

I don't agree with Prof. Alvaro that its okay to have only one user for your language. If it is useful, it should be widely used and not an obscurity.  I am not a fan of Datalog and its derivatives. I am solidly in the Prof. Wirth camp, which is that Algorithms + data structures = Programs, and i believe the weak spot in computer science is data structures, as the fundamental algorithms are now fairly well known.

A super loosely typed language like JS is going to create problems for the iDE. Since it was designed behavior that you can do crazy stuff like 1+ "21" and (is it 22 or 121 or "121")?.  The whole JS/CSS/HTML stack is fatally flawed. At no time in history did you mix 3 languages together in one source file, constantly switching back and forth between differing syntaxes, which don't even agree on how comments are formed (JS comments not equal to CSS comments). Why does CSS that can set a style have no IF statement or arithmetic, and why does JS which can do arithmetic and has IF can't draw? A horrible mess and i don't care if it is #1 on the planet, it is still a horrible mess.

Designing a widely used language means that the language designer needs to
1. put emphasis on the early stages of the learning curve, and
2. cater to common denominators of all the potential users' mind landscapes.
Designing a language for oneself means that the language design can have uncompromising focus on the functionalities that support the one specific mind landscape.

It is of course true that neither problems nor the minds that are solving them are unique _in general_; both have a lot of repetitive patterns and thus by default it makes sense to try to make tools repurposable.
But I would oppose the idea that this value of repurposability is universal enough to say that it's somehow a failure to have a language for one specific combination of mind+problem, if the language indeed fits that combination effectively.
It's not like language design is a monumental effort which must be justified in large scale to be not considered a failure.
I ride a sucky bike to store and consider the bike a success, even though it objectively is sucky on many dimensions when compared to other bikes.
It gets me there and I don't have to worry about it getting stolen even when it only has a sucky lock on it.

Friends, some days I simply don't know what we're doing.  Open your nearest JavaScript console (or whatever) and observe `350680 / 8813712 * 2203428 == 2203428 * 350680 / 8813712`.  I'm sure you all know why this is false.  But what can we do in the face of such sandy foundations?

Educate people that floating point arithmetic requires care, and you need to know the rules?
Analytic math has domains where exact numeric solutions are non-tractable (if not in theory, then in practice). So best we can do is to make the rules of floating point arithmetic deterministic and straightforward as possible and then tell people to learn those rules. Too bad they're not deterministic even in practice, on the same hardware...

<@UAL7940NM> I don't have my work documented online anywhere unfortunately. But I can try to give a quick summary here.

For the cellular boundary formula its not much. I'm essentially identifying a (conceptually) useful abstraction for this one proof and visually implementing that abstraction. That abstraction is grouping together a set of isomorphic objects for each natural number n and keeping track of the isomorphisms between them. The implementation consists of forming a new commutative diagram whose vertices/objects are now these systems of isomorphic objects and whose morphisms are now identified by specifying a particular morphism from one object of the domain system to an object of the codomain system. So now the commutative diagram reads: the map whose degree we want to take is the inclusion into the relative homology, followed by the boundary homomorphism followed by the projection onto the factor we want. This is roughly how we would understand the birds eye view of the original proof but neither this nor all the details are directly visible in the original.

For spectral sequences the representation is only a bit more substantial but the math is more difficult. For the spectral sequence of a filtered complex (of vector spaces) every vector space we will consider is uniquely isomorphic to a sub quotient of the original complex, that is, a quotient of a subspace. The relevant subspaces that we take and quotient by are linearly ordered so they can be placed on a line and then each sub quotient corresponds to a unique sub length of this line. For example if we have a filtered complex like 0=K_0 \subset K_1 \subset K_2 \subset K_3= *full complex* we can put K_0 on the line at 0, K_1 on the line at 1, K_2 on the line at 2 and K_3 at the line at three (and just have the line go from 0 to 3). Then the sub quotient K_2/K_1 would correspond to the length of line going from 1 to 2 and H_*(K_2/K_1), being a sub quotient of K_2/K_1, would be signified by a smaller length line, say from 1.2 to 1.7, for a concrete mental image (although there really isn't any basis for those or any specific numbers aside from being between 1 and 2). Also in practice I would use the span from 1 to 2 for H_*(K_2/K_1) because I would rarely consider K_2/K_1 in and of itself. This is essentially what is depicted in the first picture although we are looking at H_* of a subquotient further down, namely E_{p,q}^{r-1}. Most of the sub quotients we consider are triply indexed so its a daunting task to follow the logic for the proofs of the main theorems in a single presentation let alone get a birds eye view of it all. Its the common canvas that every development of spectral sequences paints over so I can naturally compare and contrast different approaches; I can see every development occurring simultaneously/in the same visual space. Its a really cool thing! In the second picture we see two more of the same “canvases” where the small horizontal lines indicate the subquotients we are looking at and the arrows are the morphisms in the long exact sequences relating each of the subquotients.

As I'm working to extend these techniques to representing the computation of the homology of RP^2 I'm more inspired by Bret's BART Widget than any one thing in Kill Math, since Kill Math is mostly about quantitative stuff and the widget is about this complicated highly relational thing.  (OK, I need to figure out how to include pictures).
How I represented the proof of the cellular boundary formula. (Specifically the proof found in Hatcher's book)
First picture for representing the construction of a spectral sequence from a filtered complex.

Hi <@UGMH4H202> nagle! I'm just out of grad school so I'm living at home for now.

I've heard of that study before but I've never looked at it. Its just people personally reporting how they think right? Its definitely not an either-or thing for me and I'd have a hard time even saying if I favor one way of thinking over another. It depends so much on the subject and how familiar I am with the material. I think most people are guided by the visual/kinesthetic channels for thinking but need the symbolic channels for doing rigorous math. Math is at its best imo when the symbolic and visual forms are in harmony - when the visual forms guide symbolic thinking and the symbolic forms clarify our mental images. Some parts of math are visually impoverished so to make the most out of the little visualization there is, we have to pay closer attention to the symbolic forms.

I guess thats really where my thinking is at, the parts of math that are nearly impenetrable to visual understanding, and how can you make the best of that situation. I believe things can be vastly improved here but I probably shouldn't begin that rant now.

attachments.fallback: [October 11th, 2018 3:16 AM] wtaysom: Or here's another abstraction leak: floating point arithmetic, and I don't mean `0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3`.  Today, as on most days, I was doing a linear optimization (find a solution) followed by a quadratic optimization (find the best solution).  The quadratic optimization conceptually has a feasible solution (you already have one in hand), but finding it is too much for solver.  So the fix is to cut the solver some slack, find the best solution within about 0.000000001% of the answer.

We need to become better at being humans.
We need to become better at being humans.

Learning to use symbols and knowledge in new ways, across groups, across cultures, is a powerful, valuable, and very human goal. And it is also one that is obtainable, if we only begin to open our minds to full, complete use of computers to augment our most human of capabilities.  In the computer we have a tool that does not just move earth or bend steel, but we have a tool that actually can portray symbols in new ways.  We have a tool that radically extends our capabilities in the very area that makes us most human, and most powerful.

Douglas Engelbart said that, or something close it.

I don't know if the world is going to wait for us to get it together.
I don't know if the world is going to wait for us to get it together.

One of the most interesting things on the domain of maths is the fractal-like category boundaries between provable, provably unprovable and the what-the-heck-is-I-don't-even. In computation the design tradeoffs on algorithms and data structures sometimes feel a bit similar... and, well: there's a point in real world applications where one just has to bite the bullet and accept that there's no general clean solution but it's tradeoffs all the way down.

So the best one can do in cases like these is to make sure people have the chance to see the warning signs "Ask an expert once you venture beyond here." on their way.

These decisions still seem a lot higher level than the code we write day to day. What would it take to be able to program directly in the high level concepts and decisions you list above?

Hey <@UGXH0HCSF>, ok, I’m assuming as you’re primarily focused on the research side it’s not a priority then?
I was thinking of the fact that the camera/projection system should have a good representation of the environment, so there’s some pretty good options for taking a “view” of what the system sees and projecting it onto a screen of some kind which can be manipulated with more traditional windowing methods as well as be readable by things like screen readers?

This should allow users with various accessibility issues to interact with other users with minimal problems?

It’s not a magic solution, things that are directly tied to physical objects like dynamic objects that take their cues from paper or the location of other items won’t be easily manipulatable, but it should help a lot I think?

Certainly some things could be read by screen readers, although it would be more like a “page reader” because inside RealTalk there is no “screen.” (Although there are displays in the space that, for transparency reasons, show the table or wall of pages that the camera sees.)

Certainly some things could be read by screen readers, although it would be more like a “page reader” because inside RealTalk there is no “screen.” (Although there are displays in the space that, for transparency reasons, show the table or wall of pages that the camera sees.)

One of the big design values of the project is the direct manipulation of physical objects (pages, felt tokens, etc.) that are given computational properties. So, I think there’s a lot that could be done there for accessibility. RealTalk doesn’t have speech synthesis features that I know of, so adding those would probably be a good starting point.
